subject: Physics
q: The one division of main scale of vernier callipers reads <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions of Vernier scale is equal to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions on main scale. When the two jaws of the instrument touch each other the zero of the Vernier lies to the right of zero of the main scale and its fourth division coincides with a main scale division. When a spherical bob is tightly placed between the two jaws, the zero of the Vernier scale lies in between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>6</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> Vernier division coincides with a main scale division. The diameter of the bob will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: The one division of main scale of vernier callipers reads <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions of Vernier scale is equal to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions on main scale. When the two jaws of the instrument touch each other the zero of the Vernier lies to the right of zero of the main scale and its fourth division coincides with a main scale division. When a spherical bob is tightly placed between the two jaws, the zero of the Vernier scale lies in between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>6</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> Vernier division coincides with a main scale division. The diameter of the bob will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
Given,

1 MSD=1 mm

10 VSD=9 MSD

Then, 1 VSD=910MSD=0.9 MSD

Least count of Vernier calliper is L.C. =0.1 mm=0.01 cm

Positive zero error=0.4 mm =0.04 cm

Observed reading of diameter of spherical bob=4.1 cm+6×0.01=4.16 cm

Real reading of diameter of bob=4.16-0.04=4.12 cm =412×10-2 cm

subject: Physics
q: In a screw gauge, 5 complete rotations of the screw cause it to move a linear distance of $0.25$ $\mathrm{cm}$. There are 100 circular scale divisions. The thickness of a wire measured by this screw gauge gives a reading of 4 main scale divisions and 30 circular scale divisions. Assuming negligible zero error, the thickness of the wire is:
question: In a screw gauge, 5 complete rotations of the screw cause it to move a linear distance of $0.25$ $\mathrm{cm}$. There are 100 circular scale divisions. The thickness of a wire measured by this screw gauge gives a reading of 4 main scale divisions and 30 circular scale divisions. Assuming negligible zero error, the thickness of the wire is:
solution:
Least count
$$
=\frac{\text { Value of } 1 \text { part on main scale }}{\text { Number of parts on vernier scale }}
$$
$$
=\frac{0.25}{5 \times 100} \mathrm{~cm}=5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~cm}
$$
Reading $=4 \times 0.05 \mathrm{~cm}+30 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~cm}$ $=(0.2+0.0150) \mathrm{cm}=0.2150 \mathrm{~cm}$ (Thickness of wire)

subject: Physics
q: A tiny metallic rectangular sheet has length and breadth of 5 mm and 2.5 mm , respectively. Using a specially designed screw gauge which has pitch of 0.75 mm and 15 divisions in the circular scale, you are asked to find the area of the sheet. In this measurement, the maximum fractional error will be $\frac{x}{100}$ where $x$ is _________.
question: A tiny metallic rectangular sheet has length and breadth of 5 mm and 2.5 mm , respectively. Using a specially designed screw gauge which has pitch of 0.75 mm and 15 divisions in the circular scale, you are asked to find the area of the sheet. In this measurement, the maximum fractional error will be $\frac{x}{100}$ where $x$ is _________.
solution: Since least count of the instrument can be calculated as $\begin{aligned} & \text { Least count }=\frac{\text { pitch length }}{\text { No. of division on circular scale }} \\ & =\frac{0.75}{15}=0.05 \mathrm{~mm} . \end{aligned}$ Here we are provided $\mathrm{L}=5 \mathrm{~mm} \& \mathrm{~W}=2.5 \mathrm{~mm}$ $\mathrm{L}=5 \mathrm{~mm} \& \mathrm{~W}=2.5 \mathrm{~mm}$ $\because$ We know that $\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{L} . \mathrm{W}$ For calculating fractional error, we can write $\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{~A}}=\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{~L}}+\frac{\mathrm{dW}}{\mathrm{~W}}$ Here $\mathrm{dL}=\mathrm{dW}=0.05 \mathrm{~mm}$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{~A}}=\frac{0.05}{5}+\frac{0.05}{2.5} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{~A}}=\frac{1}{100}+\frac{2}{100}=\frac{3}{100} \end{aligned}$ So, $x=3$

q: The diameter of a spherical bob is measured using a vernier callipers. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions of the main scale, in the vernier callipers, are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions of vernier scale. One main scale division is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The main scale reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>8</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of vernier scale was found to coincide exactly with one of the main scale division. If the given vernier callipers has positive zero error of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the radius of the bob is _____________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: The diameter of a spherical bob is measured using a vernier callipers. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions of the main scale, in the vernier callipers, are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions of vernier scale. One main scale division is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The main scale reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>8</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of vernier scale was found to coincide exactly with one of the main scale division. If the given vernier callipers has positive zero error of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the radius of the bob is _____________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
9 MSD=10 VSD
1 VSD=0.9 mm
1 MSD=1 mm
∴ L.C.=MSD-VSDMSD=1-0.91=0.1
Reading = Main scale reading + L.C. × vernier scale reading
=10+0.1×8
=10.8 mm

Reading of diameter=10.8 mm-0.4 mm=10.4 mm
radius of the bob=5.2 mm
=0.52 cm=52×10-2 cm

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion(A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : In Vernier calliper if positive zero error exists, then while taking measurements, the reading taken will be more than the actual reading.
Reason (R) : The zero error in Vernier Calliper might have happened due to manufacturing defect or due to rough handling.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion(A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : In Vernier calliper if positive zero error exists, then while taking measurements, the reading taken will be more than the actual reading.
Reason (R) : The zero error in Vernier Calliper might have happened due to manufacturing defect or due to rough handling.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
If a positive zero error exists in a vernier callipers, it means that the instrument shows a reading greater than zero even when the jaws are closed and there is no object being measured. In such a case, when actual measurements are taken, the readings will be more than the actual values.

The zero error in Vernier callipers can indeed occur due to manufacturing defects or rough handling. Manufacturing defects may lead to misalignments or inaccuracies in the instrument, while rough handling can result in damage that affects the calibration of the callipers.

Hence, both (A) and (R) are correct, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)

q: The least count of the main scale of a screw gauge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The minimum number of divisions on its circular scale required to measure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>μm</mi></math> diameter of a wire is:
question: The least count of the main scale of a screw gauge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The minimum number of divisions on its circular scale required to measure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>μm</mi></math> diameter of a wire is:
solution:
Least count of screw gauge =5 μm

L.C=pitchno. of div on cirxular  scale

5 μm=1 mmN

N=200

subject: Physics
q: The diameter of a sphere is measured using a vernier caliper whose 9 divisions of main scale are equal to 10 divisions of vernier scale. The shortest division on the main scale is equal to $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. The main scale reading is $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and second division of vernier scale coincides with a division on main scale. If mass of the sphere is 8.635 g, the density of the sphere is:
question: The diameter of a sphere is measured using a vernier caliper whose 9 divisions of main scale are equal to 10 divisions of vernier scale. The shortest division on the main scale is equal to $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. The main scale reading is $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and second division of vernier scale coincides with a division on main scale. If mass of the sphere is 8.635 g, the density of the sphere is:
solution: Given $9 \mathrm{MSD}=10 \mathrm{VSD}$ $\operatorname{mass}=8.635 \mathrm{~g}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-\frac{9}{10} \mathrm{MSD} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{MSD} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=0.01 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$ Reading of diameter $=\mathrm{MSR}+\mathrm{LC} \times \mathrm{VSR}$ $\begin{aligned} & =2 \mathrm{~cm}+(0.01) \times(2) \\ & =2.02 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$ Volume of sphere $=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^3=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{2.02}{2}\right)^3$ $=4.32 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ Density $=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}=\frac{8.635}{4.32}=1.998 \sim 2.00 \mathrm{~g}$

subject: Physics
q: In a Vernier calipers, when both jaws touch each other, zero of the Vernier scale is shifted to the right of zero of the main scale and $7^{\text{th}}$ Vernier division coincides with a main scale reading. If the value of $1$ main scale division is $1$ mm and there are $10$ Vernier scale divisions, then the Vernier caliper has
question: In a Vernier calipers, when both jaws touch each other, zero of the Vernier scale is shifted to the right of zero of the main scale and $7^{\text{th}}$ Vernier division coincides with a main scale reading. If the value of $1$ main scale division is $1$ mm and there are $10$ Vernier scale divisions, then the Vernier caliper has
solution:
Least count of the Vernier calipers is given by $\text{LC} = \dfrac{1 \text{ MSD}}{\text{Number of VSD}}$.

Substituting the given values, $\text{LC} = \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{10} = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ cm}$.

Since the zero of the Vernier scale is shifted to the right of the zero of the main scale, the zero error is positive.

The zero error is calculated as $+ (n \times \text{LC})$, where $n$ is the coinciding Vernier division.

Given that the $7^{\text{th}}$ Vernier division coincides with a main scale reading, $n = 7$.

Zero error $= + (7 \times 0.01 \text{ cm}) = + 0.07 \text{ cm}$.

Therefore, the Vernier caliper has a $0.07 \text{ cm}$ positive zero error.

Answer: $0.07$ cm positive zero error

subject: Physics
q: In a vernier calliper, when both jaws touch each other, zero of the vernier scale shifts towards left and its $4^{\text {th }}$ division coincides exactly with a certain division on main scale. If 50 vernier scale divisions equal to 49 main scale divisions and zero error in the instrument is $0.04 \mathrm{~mm}$ then how many main scale divisions are there in $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ ?
question: In a vernier calliper, when both jaws touch each other, zero of the vernier scale shifts towards left and its $4^{\text {th }}$ division coincides exactly with a certain division on main scale. If 50 vernier scale divisions equal to 49 main scale divisions and zero error in the instrument is $0.04 \mathrm{~mm}$ then how many main scale divisions are there in $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ ?
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& 50 \mathrm{VSD}=49 \mathrm{MSD} \\
& 1 \mathrm{VSD}=\frac{49}{50} \mathrm{MSD} \\
& \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD} \\
& \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{MSD} \cdot \frac{49}{50} \mathrm{MSD}=\frac{\mathrm{MSD}}{50} \ldots . . . . . .(1) \\
& \text {Now, } | \text { zero error }|=| 0-4(\mathrm{~L} . \mathrm{C}) | \\
& 0.04=|-4(\mathrm{~L} . \mathrm{C})| \Rightarrow \mathrm{L} . \mathrm{C}=0.01 \mathrm{~mm} \\
& \mathrm{0 . 0 4}=|-\mathrm{4}(\mathrm{L} . \mathrm{C})| \Rightarrow \mathrm{L} . \mathrm{C} \ldots . . .(2) \\
& =\mathrm{0 . 0 1} \mathrm{mm}
\end{aligned}$
Using (1) and (2)
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{M S D}{50}=0.01 \\
& M S D=0.5 \mathrm{~mm} \\
& M S D=\frac{1 \mathrm{~cm}}{\mathrm{~N}} \\
& \mathrm{~N}=\frac{1 \mathrm{~cm}}{0.5 \mathrm{~mm}}=\frac{100 \mathrm{~mm}}{5 \mathrm{~mm}} \\
& \mathrm{~N}=20
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A student in the laboratory measures thickness of a wire using screw gauge. The readings are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>23</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>19</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The percentage error is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>121</mn></mfrac><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A student in the laboratory measures thickness of a wire using screw gauge. The readings are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>23</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>19</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The percentage error is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>121</mn></mfrac><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
The average value of the readings is xavg=1.19+1.20+1.22+1.234=1.21

Now, absolute error in measurement Δxavg=0.02+0.01+0.01+0.024=0.064=0.015

Percentage error in measurement is =0.0151.21×100%=150121%

So, the value of x=150.

q:
The pitch and the number of divisions, on the circular scale, for a given screw gauge are 0.5 mm and 100 respectively. When the screw gauge is fully tightened without any object, the zero of its circular scale lies 3 divisions below the mean line.

The readings of the main scale and the circular scale, for a thin sheet, are 5.5 mm and 48 respectively, the thickness of this sheet is:
question:
The pitch and the number of divisions, on the circular scale, for a given screw gauge are 0.5 mm and 100 respectively. When the screw gauge is fully tightened without any object, the zero of its circular scale lies 3 divisions below the mean line.

The readings of the main scale and the circular scale, for a thin sheet, are 5.5 mm and 48 respectively, the thickness of this sheet is:
solution:
least count =0.5 mm100=0.005 mm

Reading =5.5 mm+48-3×0.005 mm

=5.725 mm.

subject: Physics
q: For the determination of refractive index of glass slab, a travelling microscope is used whose main scale contains 300 equal divisions equals to 15 cm. The vernier scale attached to the microscope has 25 divisions equals to 24 divisions of main scale. The least count (LC) of the travelling microscope is (in cm) :
question: For the determination of refractive index of glass slab, a travelling microscope is used whose main scale contains 300 equal divisions equals to 15 cm. The vernier scale attached to the microscope has 25 divisions equals to 24 divisions of main scale. The least count (LC) of the travelling microscope is (in cm) :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & 300 \mathrm{msd}=15 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & 1 \mathrm{msd}=\frac{15}{300} \mathrm{~cm}=0.05 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & 25 \mathrm{vsd}=24 \mathrm{msd} \\ & 1 \mathrm{vsd}=\frac{24}{25} \mathrm{msd} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{msd}-1 \mathrm{vsd}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{msd}-\frac{24}{25} \mathrm{msd}=\frac{1}{25} \mathrm{msd} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=\frac{1}{25} \times 0.05=0.002 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}$
correct option (2)

q: A student measuring the diameter of a pencil of circular cross-section with the help of a vernier scale records the following four readings <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>55</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>65</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The average of these four reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5375</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the standard deviation of the data is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>07395</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The average diameter of the pencil should therefore be recorded as :
question: A student measuring the diameter of a pencil of circular cross-section with the help of a vernier scale records the following four readings <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>55</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>65</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The average of these four reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5375</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the standard deviation of the data is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>07395</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The average diameter of the pencil should therefore be recorded as :
solution:
Significant rule says that reading should has same significant figure as that of reading given.

5.5375→ rounded to →5.54.

0.07395→ rounded to→0.07

therefore, 5.54±0.07 mm

subject: Physics
q: In a meter bridge, the wire of length 1 m has a non-uniform cross-section such that, the variation dRdl of its resistance R with length l is dRdl∝1l. Two equal resistances are connected as shown in the figure. The galvanometer has zero deflection when the jockey is at point P. What is the length AP?
question: In a meter bridge, the wire of length 1 m has a non-uniform cross-section such that, the variation dRdl of its resistance R with length l is dRdl∝1l. Two equal resistances are connected as shown in the figure. The galvanometer has zero deflection when the jockey is at point P. What is the length AP?
solution:
According to question meter bridge have non-uniform wire, where its resistance has a variation with length as dRdl∝1l 

dRdl=kl  ...1

, where k is proportionality constant
Let resistance of the left part AP and right part PB of the wire is RAP , RPB respectively.

From balanced condition of wheat stone bridge is:

PQ=RS

So, from the above condition for given meter bridge is

R'R'=RApRpB  ⇒RAp = RpB  ...2

Now from equation 1, on integrating  ∫dR=k∫dll  ...3

 for AP part of the wire of length extends from o to l from the equation 3

RAP =∫0RAP∫dR=k∫0ldll =k∫0l l-12 dl =k l-12+10l-12+1⇒RAP =2kl

Now, for PB part of the wire of length extends from l to 1 m, from the equation 3

RPB =∫0RPBdR=k∫l1dll =k∫l1 l-12 dl =k l-12+1l1-12+1⇒RPB =2k1-l 

From equation 2 RAP=RPB
∴ 2l=21-l⇒ l=1-l⇒l=12  ⇒l=14=0.25 m

subject: Physics
q: In a screw gauge when the circular scale is given five complete rotations it moves linearly by $2.5$ mm. If the circular scale has $100$ divisions, the least count of screw gauge is _____ mm.
question: In a screw gauge when the circular scale is given five complete rotations it moves linearly by $2.5$ mm. If the circular scale has $100$ divisions, the least count of screw gauge is _____ mm.
solution:
Pitch of the screw gauge is the linear distance moved in one complete rotation.

$\text{Pitch} = \dfrac{2.5}{5} = 0.5 \text{ mm}$

Least count of the screw gauge is given by the ratio of the pitch to the total number of divisions on the circular scale.

$\text{Least count} = \dfrac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{Number of divisions on circular scale}}$

$\text{Least count} = \dfrac{0.5}{100} = 0.005 \text{ mm}$

$\text{Least count} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$

Answer: $5 \times 10^{-3}$

subject: Physics
q: A travelling microscope has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> divisions per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> on the main scale while its Vernier scale has total <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn></math> Vernier scale divisions are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> main scale divisions, what is the least count of the travelling microscope ?
question: A travelling microscope has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> divisions per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> on the main scale while its Vernier scale has total <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn></math> Vernier scale divisions are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> main scale divisions, what is the least count of the travelling microscope ?
solution:
One main scale division MSD=120 cm

One vernier scale division VSD=2425MSD=2425×120 cm

∴ Least count LC=MSD-VSD=1201-2425cm

=120×125=1500=0.002 cm

=0.002 cm

subject: Physics
q: A travelling microscope is used to determine the refractive index of a glass slab. If 40 divisions are there in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> on main scale and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> Vernier scale divisions are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>49</mn></math> main scale divisions, then least count of the travelling microscope is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: A travelling microscope is used to determine the refractive index of a glass slab. If 40 divisions are there in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> on main scale and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> Vernier scale divisions are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>49</mn></math> main scale divisions, then least count of the travelling microscope is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
Given here, main scale reading, MS=140 cm

And, 50×VS=49×MS

Least count of travelling microscope is LC=MS-VS

=MS-4950 MS

=150×MS

=150×140 cm=12000×10-2 m

=5×10-6 m

subject: Physics
q:
Assertion A: If in five complete rotations of the circular scale, the distance travelled on the main scale of the screw gauge is 5 mm and there are 50 total divisions on a circular scale, then the least count is 0.001 cm.

Reason R:
Least Count = Pitch  Total divisions on circular scale 
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Assertion A: If in five complete rotations of the circular scale, the distance travelled on the main scale of the screw gauge is 5 mm and there are 50 total divisions on a circular scale, then the least count is 0.001 cm.

Reason R:
Least Count = Pitch  Total divisions on circular scale 
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is not correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is correct.

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is not correct.

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Least count = Pitch  total division on circular scale 
In 5 revolution, distance travel, 5 mm.

In 1 revolution, it will travel 1 mm.
So the least count =150=0.02

subject: Physics
q: In the experimental set up of metre bridge shown in the figure, the null point is obtaine data distance of $40 \mathrm{~cm}$ from A. If a $10 \Omega$ resistor is connected in series with $\mathrm{R}_{1},$ the null point shifts by $10 \mathrm{~cm}$. The resistance that should be connected in parallel with $\left(\mathrm{R}_{1}+10\right) \Omega$ such that the null point shifts back to its initial position is
question: In the experimental set up of metre bridge shown in the figure, the null point is obtaine data distance of $40 \mathrm{~cm}$ from A. If a $10 \Omega$ resistor is connected in series with $\mathrm{R}_{1},$ the null point shifts by $10 \mathrm{~cm}$. The resistance that should be connected in parallel with $\left(\mathrm{R}_{1}+10\right) \Omega$ such that the null point shifts back to its initial position is
solution:
Initially at null deflection $\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{2}{3}$$\ldots(\mathrm{i})$

Finally at null deflection, when null point is shifted $\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}+10}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{1}+10=\mathrm{R}_{2}$...(ii)

Solving equations (i) and (ii) we get $\frac{2 R_{2}}{3}+10=R_{2}$

$10=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}=30 \Omega$

$\& R_{1}=20 \Omega$

Now if required resistance is $\mathrm{R}$ then $\frac{\frac{30 \times \mathrm{R}}{30+\mathrm{R}}}{30}=\frac{2}{3}$

$\mathrm{R}=60 \Omega$

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment to determine the gravitational acceleration g of a place with the help of a simple pendulum, the measured time period squared is plotted against the string length of the pendulum in the figure. What is the value of g at the place?
question: In an experiment to determine the gravitational acceleration g of a place with the help of a simple pendulum, the measured time period squared is plotted against the string length of the pendulum in the figure. What is the value of g at the place?
solution:
T=2πLg

⇒   g=4π2LT2

T2L=4π2g=tanθ=slope of graph

⇒   g=4π2tanθ

∵  tanθ=8−22−0.5=61.5=4

⇒   g=4π24=(3.14)2=π2=3.142=9.87 m s-2

q: A student measured the length of a rod and wrote it as 3.50 cm. Which instrument did he use to measure it ?
question: A student measured the length of a rod and wrote it as 3.50 cm. Which instrument did he use to measure it ?
options: A vernier calliper where the 10 divisions in vernier scale matches with 9 division in main scale and main scale has 10 divisions in 1 cm
A screw gauge having 100 divisions in the circular scale and pitch as 1 mm
A screw gauge having 50 divisions in the circular scale and pitch as 1 mm
solution: As per given data the Vernier has a least count of 0.01 cm, which is consistent with the information given in question.

subject: Physics
q:
In an experiment to find out the diameter of wire using screw gauge, the following observation were noted:

(a) Screw moves 0.5 mm on main scale in one complete rotation
(b) Total divisions on circular scale =50
(c) Main scale reading is 2.5 mm
(d) 45th  division of circular scale is in the pitch line
(e) Instrument has 0.03 mm negative error Then the diameter of wire is :
question:
In an experiment to find out the diameter of wire using screw gauge, the following observation were noted:

(a) Screw moves 0.5 mm on main scale in one complete rotation
(b) Total divisions on circular scale =50
(c) Main scale reading is 2.5 mm
(d) 45th  division of circular scale is in the pitch line
(e) Instrument has 0.03 mm negative error Then the diameter of wire is :
solution:
From the data given in the question,

Pitch of the screw gauge=0.5 mm.

Total divisions on the circular scalen=50.

Therefore, the least count of the screw gauge is LC=pitchn=0.550=0.01 mm

Now, MSR=2.5 mm, CSR=45

Diameter reading =MSR+LC×CSR- zero error

⇒d=2.5+0.45--0.03

⇒d=2.98 mm

subject: Physics
q: The least count of the main scale of a vernier calipers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. Its vernier scale is divided into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions and coincide with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions of the main scale. When jaws are touching each other, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>7</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of the vernier scale coincides with a division of the main scale and the zero of vernier scale is lying right side of the zero of the main scale. When this vernier is used to measure the length of the cylinder the zero of the vernier scale between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> VSD coincides with the main scale division. The length of the cylinder is (VSD is vernier scale division)
question: The least count of the main scale of a vernier calipers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. Its vernier scale is divided into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions and coincide with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions of the main scale. When jaws are touching each other, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>7</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of the vernier scale coincides with a division of the main scale and the zero of vernier scale is lying right side of the zero of the main scale. When this vernier is used to measure the length of the cylinder the zero of the vernier scale between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> VSD coincides with the main scale division. The length of the cylinder is (VSD is vernier scale division)
solution:
Zero Error = 0+7×0.1=0.070

Vernier reading = (3.1+4×0.01) –0.07=3.07

subject: Physics
q: Least count of a vernier caliper is $\frac{1}{20 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~cm}$. The value of one division on the main scale is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the number of divisions of main scale that coincide with $\mathrm{N}$ divisions of vernier scale is :
question: Least count of a vernier caliper is $\frac{1}{20 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~cm}$. The value of one division on the main scale is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the number of divisions of main scale that coincide with $\mathrm{N}$ divisions of vernier scale is :
solution:
Least count of vernier calipers $=\frac{1}{20 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~cm}$
$\because$ Least count $=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD}$
let $\mathrm{x}$ no. of divisions of main scale coincides with $\mathrm{N}$ division of vernier scale, then
$\begin{aligned} & 1 \mathrm{VSD}=\frac{\mathrm{x} \times 1 \mathrm{~mm}}{\mathrm{~N}} \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{20 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~cm}=1 \mathrm{~mm}-\frac{\mathrm{x} \times 1 \mathrm{~mm}}{\mathrm{~N}} \\ & \frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~mm}=1 \mathrm{~mm}-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{mm} \\ & \mathrm{x}=\left(1-\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~N}}\right) \mathrm{N} \\ & \mathrm{x}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~N}-1}{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: While measuring diameter of wire using screw gauge the following readings were noted. Main scale reading is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and circular scale reading is equal to 42 divisions. Pitch of screw gauge is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and it has 100 divisions on circular scale. The diameter of the wire is $\frac{x}{50} \mathrm{~mm}$. The value of $x$ is :
question: While measuring diameter of wire using screw gauge the following readings were noted. Main scale reading is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and circular scale reading is equal to 42 divisions. Pitch of screw gauge is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and it has 100 divisions on circular scale. The diameter of the wire is $\frac{x}{50} \mathrm{~mm}$. The value of $x$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{MSR}=1 \mathrm{~mm}, \mathrm{CSR}=42, \text { pitch }=1 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=\frac{\text { pitch }}{\text { No. of CSD }}=\left(\frac{1}{100}\right)=0.01 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \text { Diameter }=\mathrm{MSR}+\mathrm{LC} \times \mathrm{CSD} \\ & \text { Diameter }=1+(0.01) \times 42 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \text { Diameter }=1.42 \mathrm{~mm}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{50} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{x}=71\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
In a screw gauge, the zero of the circular scale lies 3 divisions above the horizontal pitch line when their metallic studs are brought in contact. Using this instrument thickness of a sheet is measured. If pitch scale reading is 1 mm and the circular scale reading is 51 then the correct thickness of the sheet is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
[Assume least count is 0.01 mm ]
question:
In a screw gauge, the zero of the circular scale lies 3 divisions above the horizontal pitch line when their metallic studs are brought in contact. Using this instrument thickness of a sheet is measured. If pitch scale reading is 1 mm and the circular scale reading is 51 then the correct thickness of the sheet is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
[Assume least count is 0.01 mm ]
solution:
For a screw gauge, the measurement formula is: Reading = Pitch scale + (Circular scale × Least count) - Zero error
The zero of circular scale lies 3 divisions above the pitch line, indicating negative zero error:
Zero error = -3 × 0.01 = -0.03 mm
Reading = 1 + (51 × 0.01) - (-0.03) = 1 + 0.51 + 0.03 = 1.54 mm

subject: Physics
q:
A screw gauge of pitch 0.5 mm is used to measure the diameter of uniform wire of length 6.8 cm, the main scale reading is 1.5 mm and circular scale reading is 7. The calculated curved surface area of wire to appropriate significant figures is
[Screw gauge has 50 divisions on the circular scale]
question:
A screw gauge of pitch 0.5 mm is used to measure the diameter of uniform wire of length 6.8 cm, the main scale reading is 1.5 mm and circular scale reading is 7. The calculated curved surface area of wire to appropriate significant figures is
[Screw gauge has 50 divisions on the circular scale]
solution:
Least Count (L.C)=PN=0.5 mm50=0.01 mm

Length of wire =6.8 cm

Diameter of wire =1.5 mm+7× L.C

=1.5 mm+7×.01=1.57 mm

Curved surface area =πDl

=3.14×6.8×1.57×10-1 cm2

=3.352 cm2=3.4 cm2

subject: Physics
q: The Vernier constant of Vernier callipers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and it has zero error of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. While measuring diameter of a sphere, the main scale reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and coinciding vernier division is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> . The corrected diameter will be _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: The Vernier constant of Vernier callipers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and it has zero error of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. While measuring diameter of a sphere, the main scale reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and coinciding vernier division is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> . The corrected diameter will be _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
Vernier constant is the least count of the vernier V.C=0.1 mm=0.01 cm

zero error =-0.05 cm

∴correction=0.05 cm

M.S.R.=1.7 cm

V.S.R.=5 V.C.=5×0.01=0.05 cm

The diameter of the sphere will be,

diameter=MSR+VSR+correction

1.7+0.05+0.05=1.8 cm

=180×10-2 cm

subject: Physics
q: In a Screw Gauge, fifth division of the circular scale coincides with the reference line when the ratchet is closed. There are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions on the circular scale, and the main scale moves by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> on a complete rotation. For a particular observation the reading on the main scale is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>20</mn><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> division of the circular scale coincides with reference line. Calculate the true reading.
question: In a Screw Gauge, fifth division of the circular scale coincides with the reference line when the ratchet is closed. There are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions on the circular scale, and the main scale moves by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> on a complete rotation. For a particular observation the reading on the main scale is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>20</mn><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> division of the circular scale coincides with reference line. Calculate the true reading.
solution:
L.C. of screw Gauge=0.550=0.01 mm
Zero error of screw Gauge =5×L.C.=5×0.01=0.05 mm
Reading of screw gauge = main scale reading + circular scale reading ×L.C.-Zero error =5+20×0.01-0.05=5.15 mm
